an1.41:1.1: “Bhikkhus, just as, if a rice awn or a barley awn is wrongly set, and a person stepping on it with the hand or with the foot would not break the hand or the foot nor produce blood — that is not possible.
an1.41:1.3: Because of the awn being wrongly set, bhikkhus.
an1.41:1.4: In the same way, bhikkhus, it is not possible that a bhikkhu with a wrongly set mind would break through blindness [*avijjā*], produce knowledge [*vijjā*], and realize nibbāna.
an1.41:1.6: Because of the mind being wrongly set, bhikkhus.”
an1.42:1.1: “Bhikkhus, just as, if a rice awn or a barley awn is rightly set, and a person stepping on it with the hand or with the foot would break the hand or the foot or produce blood — that is possible.
an1.42:1.3: Because of the awn being rightly set, bhikkhus.
an1.42:1.4: In the same way, bhikkhus, it is possible that a bhikkhu with a rightly set mind would break through blindness [*avijjā*], produce knowledge [*vijjā*], and realize nibbāna.
an1.42:1.6: Because of the mind being rightly set, bhikkhus.”
an1.43:1.1: “Here, bhikkhus, I understand a certain person whose mind is corrupted, with mind knowing mind, as follows:
an1.43:1.2: ‘If this person were to die at this moment, he would be laid down after being carried to hell.’
an1.43:1.4: Because his mind, bhikkhus, is corrupted.
an1.43:1.5: And because of corruption of mind, bhikkhus, some beings here, on the breakup of the body, after death, are reborn in a state of loss, a bad destination, a lower realm, hell.”
an1.44:1.1: “Here, bhikkhus, I understand a certain person whose mind is serene, with mind knowing mind, as follows:
an1.44:1.2: ‘If this person were to die at this moment, he would be laid down after being carried to heaven.’
an1.44:1.4: Because his mind, bhikkhus, is serene.
an1.44:1.5: And because of serenity of mind, bhikkhus, some beings here, on the breakup of the body, after death, are reborn in a good destination, a heavenly world.”
an1.45:1.1: “Bhikkhus, just as if there were a water pond that is turbid, agitated, and muddy, then a person with eyes standing on the bank would not see even a tiny shell, nor a pebble, nor a school of fish moving about or standing still.
an1.45:1.3: Because the water is turbid, bhikkhus.
an1.45:1.4: In the same way, bhikkhus, it is not possible that a bhikkhu with a turbid mind would know his own good or the good of others or the good of both, or realize any higher distinction of knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones beyond the human condition.
an1.45:1.6: Because of the mind being turbid, bhikkhus.”
an1.46:1.1: “Bhikkhus, just as if there were a water pond that is clear, bright, and unturbid, then a person with eyes standing on the bank would see even a tiny shell, a pebble, a school of fish moving about or standing still.
an1.46:1.3: Because the water is unturbid, bhikkhus.
an1.46:1.4: In the same way, bhikkhus, it is possible that a bhikkhu with an unturbid mind would know his own good or the good of others or the good of both, or realize any higher distinction of knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones beyond the human condition.
an1.46:1.6: Because of the mind being unturbid, bhikkhus.”
an1.47:1.1: “Bhikkhus, whatever among all kinds of trees is the phandana tree is said to be supreme among them, that is, in softness and in pliancy.
an1.47:1.2: In the same way, bhikkhus, I do not see any other single dhamma that, when developed and made much of in this way, becomes so soft and pliant as the mind.
an1.47:1.3: When developed and made much of, bhikkhus, the mind becomes soft and pliant.”
an1.48:1.1: “Bhikkhus, I do not see any other single dhamma that turns around so quickly as the mind.
an1.48:1.2: So much so, bhikkhus, that even a simile is not easy to find for how quickly the mind turns around.”
an1.49:1.2: And it is defiled by adventitious defilements.”
an1.50:1.2: And it is freed from adventitious defilements.”
